ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general udon noodle dish class; no specific mixed tempura udon with dashi broth entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific tempura udon entry; A00LC (noodle soup dishes) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of mixed tempura udon with dashi broth, approximately 550 g, comprising udon noodles, dashi-based soup, and an assortment of tempura pieces including prawn and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Udon noodle soup with tempura (tenpura udon) — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for udon noodles (cooked), dashi broth, and tempura (prawn and vegetable), weighted by typical serving composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for mixed tempura udon with dashi broth as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked udon noodles (FDC ID 169737), dashi broth (approximated from kombu and bonito-based stock), and mixed tempura (prawn tempura FDC ID 175177 and vegetable tempura analogue). Composition weighted as approximately 45% udon noodles, 35% dashi broth, 20% mixed tempura by mass. Per-100g values derived from this weighted composite. Values are consistent with published Japanese food composition data (Standard Tables of Food Composition in Japan, 2020, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology — MEXT) for tenpura udon (item code 01-1-1-1 category reference).

gi_source.title: Udon noodles, cooked — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to mixed tempura udon dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 55 for cooked udon noodles (white wheat udon, boiled). Mixed tempura udon with dashi broth is dominated by the udon noodle component; the addition of dashi broth (negligible carbohydrate) and tempura (fat-coated, which slows gastric emptying) is expected to maintain or slightly lower the effective GI relative to plain udon. GI of 55 is applied as a conservative analogous estimate for the composite dish. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 79.75 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving at 14.5 g/100g) / 100 = 43.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi broth and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 550 g bowl provides approximately 1,760 mg sodium, which represents 76–88% of the WHO-recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth or limit portion size significantly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined wheat udon noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of ~44 per bowl, this dish poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion, substitute with wholegrain noodles if available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from deep-fried tempura
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1,760 mg per bowl) and the saturated fat contributed by deep-fried tempura batter are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose steamed or grilled protein alternatives where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Tempura Udon with Dashi Broth is a Japanese noodle soup dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat-based udon noodles served in a clear, umami-rich dashi broth, topped with an assortment of lightly battered and deep-fried tempura pieces, typically including prawn (ebi) and seasonal vegetables such as sweet potato, zucchini, or shiitake mushroom. The dashi broth is prepared by steeping kombu (dried kelp) and katsuobushi (dried bonito flakes) in hot water, producing a delicate, savoury stock that is seasoned with soy sauce and mirin. The tempura coating is made from a light wheat-flour-and-egg batter, fried to a crisp golden texture that contrasts with the soft, yielding noodles. The dish is warming, filling, and deeply associated with Japanese comfort food culture, commonly served in udon specialty restaurants and casual Japanese eateries across Japan and internationally. From a nutritional standpoint, the dish provides moderate energy (~539 kcal per bowl), meaningful protein from prawn tempura, and substantial carbohydrate from the udon noodles. The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content derived from the dashi broth and soy-based seasoning, which may be problematic for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of udon noodles is approximately 55, placing the dish at the upper boundary of the low-GI category, with a moderate glycemic load per serving relevant to Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish contains wheat gluten, crustacean shellfish, fish-derived dashi, soy, and egg allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for mixed tempura udon with dashi broth as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked udon noodles, dashi broth, and mixed tempura, weighted by typical serving composition. The GI value is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked udon noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this composite estimate and is not a primary nutrient of concern for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for the component-based estimation. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in the component sources (udon noodles, vegetable-oil-fried tempura). The dish is served at GyoGyo - Funan (field survey data listing, food_id GD004144) at SGD 18.53. The menu item name '607D Mixed Tempura Udon With Dashi Broth' uses a restaurant-internal code prefix '607D'; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Mixed Tempura Udon with Dashi Broth'. Halal status is not confirmed as dashi is typically made from bonito (fish), which is permissible, but restaurant-level halal certification for GyoGyo - Funan has not been verified. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 × 14.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 550 g serving) / 10000 = 43.8.
